steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: This is a Words in Context question asking what Wong means by “composing.” The decisive clue is her own explanation: “By doing ‘nothing’ and daydreaming, I am actually composing, even if words don’t immediately materialize” — she equates composing with the mental creative work that happens before any words are written.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘She is deceiving herself about her true feelings about a subject.’: Wong makes no suggestion of self-deception; she presents daydreaming as genuinely productive, not as a rationalization. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘She is engaging in an important part of her writing process.’: This matches the passage directly — Wong describes how a vibe, a phrase, or an epiphany can emerge during apparent idleness, showing that daydreaming is real creative work within her process. ✓
  • Choice C — ‘She is gathering sources of information to use in her writing.’: The passage mentions watching or reading “something brilliant” as one possible trigger, but gathering external sources is not what “composing” denotes here; the emphasis is on internal mental creation. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘She is being soothed by immersing herself in creative works by other people.’: Being soothed describes a passive emotional effect; Wong argues the opposite — that she is actively (if invisibly) creating, not merely being comforted. ✗
That confirms B is correct.
